context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on the City Case Study Investigation draft, and I’m kind of stuck on turning my research into 2 actual climate action plan components. Could you help me figure out how to structure one component so I’m not just listing ideas?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through this! Before we dig in — what city are you working with, and what did your SWOT analysis reveal as the most pressing vulnerability or challenge? Start there and we'll build from what you already know.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help getting started. Rather than explaining the structure for them, I should ask them to share what they already have — their city, their SWOT findings, and any initial ideas — so I can guide them from their own material. This follows the Socratic method and guided discovery principles. I want to see what they bring to the table before offering any scaffolding.

exchanges.student: I’m using Phoenix, and my SWOT kept pointing back to extreme heat and water insecurity. I think the biggest challenge is that the impacts aren’t equal across the city, but I’m not sure how to turn that into a focused policy component instead of something way too broad.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp observation — the equity angle is actually central to this assignment, not a complication. So let's focus it: between extreme heat and water insecurity, which one do you want to tackle first? And when you think about who bears the worst of that impact in Phoenix, which communities come to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a strong and specific tension: unequal distribution of climate impacts in Phoenix. This is exactly the kind of insight that should anchor a policy component. Rather than explaining how to narrow it down for them, I should ask them to think about which of the two issues (heat or water) they want to focus on first, and push them to think about WHO is most affected — this will naturally lead them toward a focused, EJ-grounded component. One targeted question is enough here.

exchanges.student: I think extreme heat makes more sense first. The communities I’m thinking of are low-income neighborhoods, older adults, outdoor workers, and probably areas with less tree cover, but I feel like I’m naming groups without connecting them to an actual policy yet.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've got the 'who.' Now think about the 'why.' What is it specifically about those neighborhoods — physically or structurally — that makes extreme heat worse for the people living there? If you can name that, a policy direction tends to follow naturally.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified vulnerable populations but is stuck on the bridge between identifying who is affected and proposing what to do about it. Rather than giving them a policy idea, I should ask them to think about the mechanism — what is it about those neighborhoods that makes heat worse, and what could realistically change that? This will help them discover a policy direction on their own. One focused question is enough.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s things like urban heat island effects, more pavement, less shade, and older housing that maybe costs more to cool. Also some people can’t just stay inside or pay higher energy bills. I’m not fully sure if that means my policy should be about trees, cooling centers, housing retrofits, or all of it, which is where I’m getting stuck.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually diagnosed the problem really well. Here's something worth sitting with: the assignment asks for focused policy components, not a full plan. Looking at the root causes you just named — heat island effects, lack of shade, costly cooling — which single intervention do you think gets at the most of those problems at once for the communities you mentioned?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done great diagnostic work identifying the physical and structural drivers of heat vulnerability. Now they're stuck choosing between multiple interventions. Rather than telling them which one to pick, I should help them realize that the assignment asks for focused components (not comprehensive plans), and nudge them to think about which single intervention best addresses the root causes they just identified. One question pointing them toward prioritization is the right move.

exchanges.student: Maybe housing retrofits? Trees help, but they take time, and cooling centers feel more like emergency response than fixing the underlying issue. I’m still unsure though, because retrofits sound expensive and maybe too big for one component.
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning for ruling out the others is actually quite solid. On the retrofit concern — what if the policy didn't have to apply to every home in Phoenix? Who specifically would you target first, and why? That kind of scoping might be what makes it manageable as a single component.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good analytical reasoning — they're weighing tradeoffs between interventions, which is exactly the kind of thinking needed. Their hesitation about retrofits being 'too big' is worth probing, because scope is about how you frame the policy, not the intervention itself. I should push them to think about how a policy recommendation can be targeted and specific even within a large intervention area. One nudging question is enough.

exchanges.student: Okay, that helps. I’d probably target low-income households in the hottest census tracts, maybe renters in older multifamily buildings too, because they have less control and higher exposure. I think I’m seeing it better now, but I’m not sure how to phrase the opportunity vs. the recommendation without repeating myself.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to notice the distinction matters. Think about it this way: what's the difference between a door being open and deciding to walk through it? How might that map onto 'Opportunity' versus 'Recommendation'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done excellent work narrowing their focus and identifying a clear target population with good reasoning. Now they're asking about the structural difference between 'Opportunity' and 'Policy Recommendation' sections. This is a legitimate question about the assignment structure. Rather than explaining it for them, I should ask them to think about what each word means — opportunity is about what makes action possible NOW, recommendation is about what you actually propose to DO. One question to draw out that distinction.

exchanges.student: I think the opportunity is like the opening Phoenix already has — maybe existing heat planning, federal/state funding, or available building efficiency programs. Then the recommendation is the actual thing I’m proposing, like a targeted heat-resilience retrofit program for low-income and renter-heavy buildings. Is that the right split?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's exactly the right split — well reasoned. You now have your target population, your root cause analysis, your opportunity framing, and your policy direction. I think you have enough to start writing a draft of the recommendation section. Give it a go — what's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly grasped the distinction between Opportunity and Recommendation. This is a good moment to affirm their reasoning briefly and then push them forward toward actually drafting something, since we've been discussing this long enough. The assignment requires 200-300 words for the recommendation — they have the ingredients, now they need to write. I should nudge them toward a draft attempt rather than continuing to discuss structure.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “Phoenix should create a targeted heat-resilience retrofit program for low-income households and older multifamily rental buildings in the city’s hottest neighborhoods.” Does that sound specific enough, or is it still too generic?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid start. Now ask yourself: if someone read only that sentence, what would they still not know about how this program actually works? What's missing that would make it feel concrete rather than general?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate their sentence, which risks me doing their writing work for them. I should not suggest language or polish their work. However, I can ask them a question that helps them self-evaluate its specificity — pushing them to think about what details a reader would still need to understand the proposal. This keeps them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: I think I’m missing what the retrofits actually include, how places get prioritized, and maybe who pays. I’m getting a little tangled trying to make it concrete without turning it into a giant list. Could you help me figure out the 2–3 most important details to add next?
exchanges.tutor: You just listed three strong candidates yourself — what retrofits include, how places get prioritized, and who pays. Given that your component is grounded in environmental justice, which of those three do you think matters most to your argument? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to select the 2-3 most important details for them, which would mean doing their thinking for them. I should not do that. However, they've already identified three good candidates themselves (what retrofits include, prioritization criteria, funding). I should point that out and ask them to decide which matter most given their EJ focus — this keeps them in the driver's seat and uses their own words against their self-doubt.

exchanges.student: I think prioritization matters most, because that’s where the EJ part really shows up. So I’d want to say the program should prioritize the hottest census tracts and buildings housing low-income residents, older adults, or medically vulnerable people. I’m a bit less sure how to mention funding without derailing the paragraph.
exchanges.tutor: Strong reasoning on prioritization. On funding — look back at the component structure in the assignment. Is 'who pays' necessarily a Recommendation detail, or could it fit better somewhere else in the component? Once you've thought that through, I think you have enough to write a full draft of your first component. Try it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong analytical choice and can articulate their reasoning well. On the funding question, they're worried about derailing the paragraph — this is actually a good structural instinct. I should ask them to think about what role funding plays in the component sections they have available (Opportunity vs. Recommendation vs. EJ Implications vs. Interactions) rather than telling them where it goes. This keeps them problem-solving independently. We're also getting close to the point where they should just write — I should nudge them toward drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all 10 exchanges. No direct answers or submission-ready content were provided; instead, the tutor consistently used targeted questions to guide the student toward their own conclusions. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building progressively from vulnerability identification to policy scoping to drafting. No off-topic drift, no redundancy, and communication was consistently concise and supportive.
grade.judge_reasoning: Across all 10 turns, the tutor never gave the student a final answer or near-submission-ready content — every key idea (target population, policy type, opportunity vs. recommendation framing, prioritization logic) was generated by the student through guided questioning. Scaffolding was strong: the tutor acknowledged student progress at each step and built on it rather than restarting. Meta-learning feedback was present and specific (e.g., Turn 5 affirming the student's reasoning process for ruling out alternatives, Turn 9 redirecting the student back to their own list of candidates). Dialogue quality was excellent — no spiraling, no off-topic engagement, and the tutor re-anchored to the assignment structure in Turn 10 by pointing the student back to the component format. Responses were appropriately brief and interactive throughout, and tone was consistently warm and formative without implying grading authority.
